metadata.purpose: Lab-value interpretation reference for IDCs (Independent Duty Corpsmen).
metadata.scope: Adult ambulatory labs commonly encountered in IDC sick call. Lab interpretation only — no operational/duty-status content.
metadata.interval_convention: [min, max) — inclusive low, exclusive high. Adjacent ranges share boundaries cleanly.
metadata.unknown_context_policy: When sex/age not provided for a context-stratified lab, falls back to 'default' band set with conservative boundaries; output flags the assumption.
metadata.bracket_placeholders: Square-bracket placeholders like [findings], [med list] are NOT substituted — they are intentional fill-ins for the IDC.
metadata.disclaimer: Clinical decision support. Not a substitute for clinical judgment. Use de-identified test data only.

Critical Low.category: Critical hypokalemia (<2.5 mEq/L) — high arrhythmia risk
next_tests: 12-lead ECG (U waves, ST depression, T-wave flattening, ventricular ectopy).
Magnesium — refractory hypokalemia is often hypomagnesemic.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Critical Low). Stat 12-lead ECG obtained — [findings]. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV potassium replacement and continuous cardiac monitoring. Held: [diuretics, laxatives, beta-agonists, insulin]. Mg level pending.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a critically low potassium level. This can cause dangerous heart rhythm problems. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone or feel unwell. Bring this notification with you to the ED.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Severe Low). ECG obtained — [findings]. Plan: oral KCl 40–80 mEq with close follow-up (or IV repletion if symptomatic / unable to tolerate PO); check Mg and replete if low; review meds (diuretics, laxatives, GI losses). Recheck K within 24–48 h. Patient counseled to return for chest pain, palpitations, severe weakness, or worsening symptoms.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a low potassium level. We need to replace it with a potassium supplement. Please come to the clinic today to start treatment, and we will recheck the level in 1–2 days. Call us right away or go to the emergency department if you have chest pain, a fast or irregular heartbeat, or severe muscle weakness.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Mild Low). Plan: dietary repletion (banana, orange juice, potatoes, leafy greens); review diuretic use, GI losses; check Mg if not done; recheck K in 1–2 weeks.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a slightly low potassium level. Eat potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, leafy greens). We will recheck the level in 1–2 weeks. Call the clinic if you have weakness, cramping, or palpitations.

next_tests: Repeat K to rule out hemolyzed sample.
Review medications (ACEi/ARB, K-sparing, NSAIDs, K supplements).
Mild High.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Mild High). Likely contributors: [hemolysis, ACEi/ARB, K-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs, K supplements, dietary]. Plan: repeat K (non-hemolyzed); medication review; recheck within 1–2 weeks.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your potassium level is slightly above normal. This can sometimes happen because of medications, diet, or how the blood was drawn. We will recheck the level. Continue your usual medications unless we tell you otherwise.

next_tests: Repeat K stat (rule out hemolyzed sample).
Moderate High.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Moderate High). Stat 12-lead ECG obtained — [findings]. Plan: hold K-elevating medications (ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, NSAIDs, K-sparing diuretics, K supplements); repeat K within hours; if confirmed and ECG abnormal or symptoms present — refer to ED.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your potassium level is moderately high. Please come to the clinic today for an EKG and a repeat blood test. Hold any potassium supplements until told otherwise. Go to the emergency department if you have chest pain, palpitations, or muscle weakness.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperkalemia (6.0–6.9 mEq/L) — admission warranted
next_tests: Stat repeat K (do not delay treatment).
Severe High.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Severe High). Stat 12-lead ECG obtained — [findings]. All K-elevating medications held. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV calcium, insulin/dextrose, and definitive management with monitored admission.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a dangerously high potassium level. This can affect your heart rhythm. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone or feel unwell. Bring this notification with you to the ED.

next_tests: 12-lead ECG immediately (peaked T, widened QRS, sine wave).
Critical High.ehr_plan: K = {value} mEq/L (Critical High). Stat 12-lead ECG obtained — [findings: peaked T, QRS widening, sine wave]. Emergent treatment initiated as indicated: IV calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization, insulin/dextrose, beta-agonist nebulizer; consider sodium bicarbonate if acidotic; arrange dialysis as appropriate. All K-elevating medications held. Activated EMS / direct ED transport.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your potassium level is at a life-threatening level. We are arranging emergency transport. If you are reading this on your own, call 911 immediately or go to the nearest emergency department. Do not eat or drink. Do not drive yourself.

Critical Low.category: Critical hyponatremia (<120 mEq/L) — risk of seizure / coma
next_tests: Serum and urine osmolality, urine sodium.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Critical Low). Mental status: [alert/confused/obtunded]. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for hypertonic saline (3% NaCl), monitored sodium correction (target 4–6 mEq/L rise in first 4 h, ≤10–12 mEq/L in 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination), and workup of underlying cause.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a critically low sodium level. This can cause seizures or coma. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone, confused, or feel unwell.

next_tests: Serum and urine osmolality, urine sodium.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Severe Low). Symptoms: [headache, nausea, confusion, gait]. If symptomatic or acute drop — refer to ED for monitored correction. If asymptomatic and chronic, evaluate volume status (hypo/eu/hypervolemic) and treat underlying cause; correction rate 6–12 mEq/L in 24 h, ≤18 in 48 h.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a low sodium level that needs prompt evaluation. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department if you have a headache, nausea, confusion, weakness, or trouble walking.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Moderate Low). Asymptomatic. Plan: workup for cause (volume status, urine studies, TSH/cortisol if indicated); medication review; fluid restriction if SIADH suspected; recheck within days.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a moderately low sodium level. We will look into possible causes and may adjust your medications or fluid intake. Please come to the clinic for follow-up. Call us or go to the ED if you develop confusion, severe headache, or seizures.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Mild Low). Asymptomatic. Plan: medication review, hydration counseling, recheck in 1–2 weeks.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your sodium level is slightly low. This is often related to medications or fluid intake. We will recheck it. Continue your usual diet and medications unless we tell you otherwise.

Mild High.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Mild High). Plan: assess hydration, encourage free water intake, review meds (diuretics); recheck in 1–2 weeks.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your sodium level is slightly high — usually a sign of mild dehydration. Drink water throughout the day. We will recheck the level.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Moderate High). Plan: aggressive oral or IV free water replacement; identify cause (insufficient intake, GI losses, DI); correct gradually (≤10–12 mEq/L per 24 h); recheck within 24 h.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your sodium level is moderately high, suggesting significant dehydration. Please come to the clinic today for evaluation and fluids. Go to the emergency department if you feel confused, very weak, or unable to drink fluids.

Severe High.category: Severe hypernatremia (≥160 mEq/L)
Severe High.ehr_plan: Na = {value} mEq/L (Severe High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV free water replacement and monitored correction (≤10–12 mEq/L per 24 h to avoid cerebral edema), workup for diabetes insipidus or other cause.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a dangerously high sodium level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone or feel unwell.

glucose.context_policy: Diabetic context (yes/no) is asked in the patient sidebar. Non-diabetic with hyperglycemia at Moderate High or worse is treated as new-onset diabetes presenting acutely — ED evaluation. Diabetic with hyperglycemia uses anion gap (when available) to determine acuity for DKA risk.

next_tests: Insulin / C-peptide, sulfonylurea screen, AM cortisol (no diabetes context — workup for cause).
Consider islet cell tumor, adrenal insufficiency, or factitious cause.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Critical Low). Patient is NOT known diabetic — concerning for occult cause (insulinoma, sulfonylurea exposure, adrenal insufficiency, sepsis, hepatic failure). Mental status: [alert/altered/unresponsive]. Treatment: 15 g rapid carbohydrate PO if able, or IV D50 / IM glucagon if unable. Recheck glucose 15 min. Patient instructed to proceed to the nearest emergency department for monitored evaluation and workup of cause.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is dangerously low. Because you are not a known diabetic, this is unusual and we need to find the cause. If you can swallow safely, eat or drink something with quick sugar (juice, regular soda, glucose tablets) immediately. Please go to the nearest emergency department now for evaluation, or call 911 if you are confused, weak, or unable to swallow.

next_tests: Insulin / C-peptide if recurrent or unexplained; AM cortisol; TSH; LFTs.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low) in non-diabetic patient. Plan: 15 g fast-acting carbs, recheck glucose in 15 min; if recurrent or unexplained — workup for cause (insulinoma, factitious, adrenal insufficiency, hepatic failure); consider ED referral if symptomatic or recurrent.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is low. Because you are not a known diabetic, we need to look into the cause. Eat or drink 15 grams of fast-acting sugar (juice, glucose tablets) and recheck. Please come to the clinic for further evaluation, or go to the emergency department if you cannot keep your sugar up, feel confused, or have repeated low readings.

Mild High.category: Impaired fasting glucose / prediabetes (100–125 mg/dL fasting)
next_tests: Repeat fasting glucose if not confirmed.
Mild High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Mild High; prediabetes range if fasting). Plan: confirm fasting status; check A1C; lifestyle counseling (diet, weight, ≥150 min/week activity); recheck glucose and A1C in 3–6 months. Consider metformin for high-risk patients per ADA.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is slightly high. This can mean prediabetes. Lifestyle changes (healthy eating, regular activity, weight management) are very effective. We will check a hemoglobin A1C and recheck your sugar in a few months.

Moderate High.category: Hyperglycemia in diabetes range (126–199 mg/dL) — non-diabetic
next_tests: Lipid panel, urine albumin/Cr ratio (new-diabetes baseline).
Moderate High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High) in patient NOT known diabetic — likely new-onset diabetes presenting acutely. Plan: patient instructed to proceed to the nearest emergency department for evaluation, BMP with anion gap, ketones, and initiation of treatment. New-diabetes baseline labs (A1C, lipids, microalbumin) and education to follow.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is high enough to suggest new diabetes. Because you are not a known diabetic, we need you to be evaluated promptly. Please go to the nearest emergency department today for further testing and to start treatment. Bring this notification with you.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperglycemia (200–299 mg/dL) — non-diabetic
Severe High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Severe High) in patient NOT known diabetic — concern for new-onset diabetes presenting acutely with possible DKA/HHS. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV fluids, insulin, electrolyte correction, and DKA/HHS workup.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is very high. Because you are not a known diabetic, this is a medical emergency — you may be developing a serious complication of new diabetes. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911.

next_tests: UA, lactate, infection workup if febrile.
Critical High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Critical High) in patient NOT known diabetic — likely new-onset diabetes presenting with DKA / HHS. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV fluids, insulin therapy, electrolyte correction, and DKA/HHS workup.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is at a dangerous level. This is a medical emergency. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911.

Critical Low.category: Severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL) — known diabetic, ADA Level 2/3
next_tests: Review diabetes regimen — insulin dose/timing, sulfonylurea use, recent meal/activity.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Critical Low) in known diabetic. Likely medication-related (insulin, sulfonylurea). Mental status: [alert/altered/unresponsive]. Treatment: 15 g rapid carbohydrate PO if able, or IV D50 / IM glucagon if unable. Hold next dose of suspected medication; review and adjust regimen. Refer to ED if recurrent within 24 h, severe symptoms, or unable to maintain glucose despite repletion.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is dangerously low. If you can swallow safely, eat or drink something with quick sugar (juice, regular soda, glucose tablets) immediately. Hold your next dose of insulin or diabetes pills until we review your regimen. Call us right away. Go to the emergency department or call 911 if you cannot keep your sugar up, feel confused, or have repeated low readings.

Severe Low.category: Hypoglycemia (54–69 mg/dL) — known diabetic, ADA Level 1 alert
next_tests: Review diabetes meds (sulfonylureas, insulin) and dosing, recent meals, activity.
Consider A1C trend and continuous glucose monitor data.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low / ADA Level 1) in known diabetic. Plan: 15-15 rule (15 g carb, recheck in 15 min). Review and adjust diabetes regimen — likely de-intensify insulin/sulfonylurea. Counsel on hypoglycemia awareness and glucagon prescription if recurrent.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is low. Eat or drink 15 grams of fast-acting sugar (4 oz juice, 3 glucose tablets, or 1 tbsp honey), then recheck your sugar in 15 minutes. We will review your diabetes medications and likely adjust them. Call us if this happens again.

Mild High.category: Mildly elevated glucose in known diabetic — within typical range for many
next_tests: Continuous glucose data review if available.
Mild High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Mild High) in known diabetic. No urgent intervention. Plan: A1C if not recent; assess overall glycemic control and adjust regimen as appropriate at scheduled visit.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is slightly above target. This is common in diabetes. Continue your usual medications and routine. We will review your A1C and overall control at your next visit.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High) in known diabetic. Plan: A1C; review diabetes regimen — adherence, dose, timing; lifestyle review; intensify regimen as appropriate per ADA Standards of Care.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is moderately high for someone with diabetes. We will check your A1C and review your medications and routine. Call us if you feel unwell, especially with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or unusual fatigue.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperglycemia in known diabetic (200–299 mg/dL) — anion-gap-driven acuity
Severe High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Severe High) in known diabetic. Anion gap = [computed]. Plan: if AG >12 OR ketones positive OR symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations) — patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for DKA workup and IV management. If AG normal and asymptomatic — hydration, regimen adjustment, close follow-up; consider sliding-scale insulin and recheck glucose. Note: euglycemic DKA possible on SGLT2i — assess for acidosis even with lower glucose.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is significantly high. Drink water (not sugary drinks). If you feel nauseated, are vomiting, have belly pain, or are breathing rapidly, go to the nearest emergency department right away — these can be signs of diabetic ketoacidosis. Otherwise, please come to the clinic today for further evaluation.

Critical High.category: Critical hyperglycemia in known diabetic (≥300 mg/dL) — DKA / HHS concern
next_tests: UA, lactate, infection workup if febrile.
Critical High.ehr_plan: Glucose = {value} mg/dL (Critical High) in known diabetic. Anion gap = [computed]. Concerns: DKA / HHS. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV fluids, insulin therapy, electrolyte correction, and DKA/HHS workup. Note: euglycemic DKA can occur at glucose <200 mg/dL on SGLT2 inhibitors.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your blood sugar is at a level that is dangerous, especially if you have signs of a diabetic emergency (nausea, vomiting, belly pain, fast deep breathing, fruity breath, confusion). Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911.

creatinine.trend_note: When a baseline creatinine is available, prefer KDIGO AKI staging — Stage 1: +0.3 mg/dL within 48 h or 1.5–1.9× baseline within 7 d; Stage 2: 2.0–2.9× baseline; Stage 3: ≥3× baseline, ≥4.0 mg/dL, or RRT initiation. Phase 1 uses the static thresholds below; trend computation is added in Phase 4 timeline UI. When no baseline is known, use the `differentiation` block for AKI vs CKD vs AKI-on-CKD reasoning.

differentiation.purpose: Distinguish AKI vs CKD vs AKI-on-CKD; assign CKD G/A stage (KDIGO) when data permits; recommend chronic CKD lab panel at G3a or worse.
differentiation.render_as: Sidebar inputs — last known creatinine + approximate date + urine albumin/Cr ratio. Engine computes eGFR (CKD-EPI 2021), KDIGO AKI stage if baseline supplied, and CKD G_A_ stage. UI shows computed values + acute/chronic feature reference + workup implications.

baseline_input.last_creatinine_help: Most recent prior creatinine. If supplied, engine computes KDIGO AKI staging.
baseline_input.last_creatinine_date_label: Approximate date of last known creatinine
baseline_input.urine_acr_help: If supplied with current Cr + age + sex, engine assigns full CKD G_A_ stage.

differentiation.egfr_formula: CKD-EPI 2021 (no race coefficient). Requires age + sex.

G1.label: G1 — eGFR ≥90, normal/high (with markers of kidney damage)

G3a.label: G3a — eGFR 45–59, mildly to moderately decreased

G3b.label: G3b — eGFR 30–44, moderately to severely decreased

A1.label: A1 — UACR <30 mg/g, normal to mildly increased

chronic_lab_panel: Urine albumin/creatinine ratio (UACR) — if not done

missing_data_guidance.no_age_sex: eGFR cannot be computed without age and sex — set in the patient context sidebar to enable CKD staging.
missing_data_guidance.no_baseline: AKI staging cannot be computed without a prior creatinine. If acute presentation is suspected, treat as AKI and obtain prior labs from EHR / outside records.
missing_data_guidance.no_acr: CKD A-stage (albuminuria) cannot be assigned without urine albumin/Cr ratio. Order UACR to complete CKD staging.
missing_data_guidance.all_missing: If neither baseline Cr nor UACR is available and the presentation could be acute, prioritize AKI workup (UA with microscopy, FENa if needed, renal ultrasound, medication review).

acute_pattern_features: Recent volume loss, sepsis, or hemorrhage
chronic_pattern_features: Established HTN, DM, family history of kidney disease
Anemia, hyperphosphatemia, secondary hyperPTH (chronic uremic milieu)
Small or echogenic kidneys on prior imaging
Stable elevated creatinine on prior labs
differentiation.mixed_pattern_note: AKI-on-CKD = established CKD risk factors PLUS a recent acute trigger. Manage the acute phase as AKI; re-establish CKD baseline after stabilization.

workup_implications.acute_pattern: AKI workup — volume status, urinalysis with microscopy, urine sodium / FENa if pre-renal vs intrinsic uncertain, renal ultrasound if obstruction possible, medication review (hold nephrotoxins).
workup_implications.chronic_pattern: CKD staging — assign G_A_ from eGFR + UACR; at G3a or worse, order chronic CKD lab panel (Hgb, Ca, P, iPTH, 25-OH D, HCO3, lipids, UACR).
workup_implications.mixed_pattern: Both — address AKI urgency first; once stabilized, re-establish CKD baseline and stage.

Mild High.category: Mildly elevated creatinine — compare to baseline
Mild High.ehr_plan: Cr = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). eGFR pending/calculated. Compare to baseline: [baseline value, date]. If new elevation — assess hydration, hold nephrotoxic meds, UA, urine ACR; recheck in 1–2 weeks. If chronic — establish CKD staging and risk stratification; lifestyle and BP/glycemic optimization.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your kidney function test is slightly elevated. We will compare it to past results, check a urine sample, and may adjust medications that can affect the kidneys. Drink adequate water and avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) until told otherwise.

Moderate High.category: Moderately elevated creatinine — concerning for AKI or CKD progression
Moderate High.ehr_plan: Cr = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). eGFR [computed]. Compare to baseline: [baseline value, date]. Plan: hold nephrotoxic meds (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, contrast pending eval); UA, urine ACR; CMP including K (rule out hyperkalemia); recheck within days. If acute rise meets KDIGO AKI criteria — referral to nephrology and consider hospital admission.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your kidney function test is moderately elevated. Please come to the clinic this week for further evaluation and a repeat blood test. Stop NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) until we tell you otherwise. Drink water normally. Call us or go to the emergency department if you have decreased urination, swelling, shortness of breath, or confusion.

Severe High.category: Severely elevated creatinine — likely AKI or advanced CKD
Severe High.ehr_plan: Cr = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). eGFR [computed, low]. Stat K and acid/base check given AKI risk. Patient instructed to proceed to the emergency department or arrange same-day nephrology evaluation for AKI workup, fluid/electrolyte management, and consideration of admission.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your kidney function test is significantly elevated. Please go to the emergency department or come to the clinic immediately for evaluation. Do not take NSAIDs. Bring this notification with you.

Critical High.category: Critical creatinine elevation (≥4.0 mg/dL) — likely severe AKI / RRT consideration
Critical High.ehr_plan: Cr = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Stat BMP and acid/base. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for AKI workup, hyperkalemia/acidosis management, and nephrology consultation for possible renal replacement therapy.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your kidney function test is at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone, very weak, or have decreased urination, swelling, or trouble breathing.

sources: KDIGO 2012 AKI guideline (2026 update in draft)

calcium.albumin_correction_note: Albumin-corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 − albumin). Engine applies this in evaluate_panel when calcium and albumin are both in the input set; UI surfaces the corrected value and severity inline on the calcium card.

Critical Low.category: Critical hypocalcemia (<7.0 mg/dL) — tetany / seizure risk
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Critical Low). Albumin-corrected Ca: [computed]. ECG obtained — [QT, findings]. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV calcium gluconate, telemetry, and workup for cause (hypoparathyroidism, severe hypomagnesemia, vitamin D deficiency).
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your calcium level is critically low. This can cause muscle spasms, seizures, or heart rhythm problems. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low). Symptoms: [perioral numbness, tetany, Chvostek/Trousseau, cramping]. If symptomatic — refer to ED for IV calcium. If asymptomatic — oral calcium + vitamin D, replete Mg, identify cause; recheck within days.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your calcium level is significantly low. Please come to the clinic today. We will start a calcium and vitamin D supplement. Go to the emergency department if you have muscle spasms, tingling around the mouth or fingers, or seizures.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Moderate Low). Albumin-corrected Ca: [computed]. Plan: oral calcium + vitamin D supplementation; replete Mg if low; PTH/vitamin D workup; recheck in 1–2 weeks.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your calcium level is moderately low. We will start a supplement and check vitamin D and parathyroid hormone levels. Call us if you develop muscle spasms or numbness around your mouth or hands.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Mild Low). Albumin-corrected Ca: [computed]. Often factitious due to low albumin. Plan: vitamin D level; dietary counseling; recheck in 1–2 weeks.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your calcium level is slightly low. This is often related to low albumin or vitamin D. We will recheck the level and may start a supplement.

Mild High.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Albumin-corrected Ca: [computed]. Plan: PTH-driven workup (primary hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, vitamin D excess, thiazides, immobilization); review medications; recheck.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your calcium level is slightly high. We will check additional tests to find the cause. Stay well hydrated. Call us if you develop nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, severe constipation, or confusion.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Albumin-corrected Ca: [computed]. Symptoms: [stones, bones, abdominal groans, psychic moans]. If symptomatic — refer to ED for aggressive saline hydration. If asymptomatic — initiate workup, push fluids, hold thiazides/Ca/Vit D; close follow-up.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your calcium level is moderately high. Please come to the clinic today. Drink plenty of water. Go to the emergency department if you have severe nausea, vomiting, confusion, or weakness.

Severe High.category: Severe hypercalcemia (14.0–15.9 mg/dL) — hypercalcemic crisis
Severe High.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Severe High; hypercalcemic crisis). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for aggressive IV saline hydration, calcitonin, bisphosphonates, and workup for primary hyperparathyroidism vs malignancy. Hold all calcium, vitamin D, thiazides.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your calcium level is dangerously high. This is a medical emergency. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself. Stop any calcium or vitamin D supplements until told otherwise.

Critical High.ehr_plan: Ca = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV saline, calcitonin, bisphosphonates, and consideration of hemodialysis. EMS activated as appropriate.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your calcium level is at a life-threatening level. Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

hemoglobin.pregnancy_note: Anemia in pregnancy is Hgb <11 g/dL (T1/T3) or <10.5 (T2) per ACOG. Engine uses 11 as the Normal floor across all trimesters — slightly conservative for T2 to avoid missed iron deficiency. Plasma volume expansion lowers Hgb physiologically; supplemental iron is recommended in pregnancy.

Critical Low.category: Critical anemia (Hgb <7.0 g/dL) — AABB transfusion threshold
next_tests: CBC w/ diff, retic count, peripheral smear.
Iron studies, B12, folate, LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin (hemolysis workup).
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Critical Low). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for type & screen, transfusion consideration per AABB (restrictive threshold <7 g/dL), and workup for cause (acute bleed vs hemolysis vs marrow failure).
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a critically low red blood cell count (anemia). This may need a blood transfusion. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself — have someone drive you, or call 911 if you are alone or feel unwell. Watch for chest pain, shortness of breath, or fainting.

next_tests: CBC w/ diff, retic, peripheral smear.
Hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin) if indicated.
Stool occult blood; consider GI evaluation.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Severe Low). Symptoms: [fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, chest pain, lightheadedness]. If symptomatic, hemodynamic compromise, or active bleeding — refer to ED. Otherwise: full anemia workup, treat underlying cause; close follow-up; transfusion if symptomatic or threshold met.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a significantly low red blood cell count. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department right away if you have chest pain, shortness of breath, or feel like you may pass out.

next_tests: CBC w/ diff, retic, peripheral smear.
MCV-guided workup: <80 → iron studies; 80–100 → renal function, retic; >100 → B12, folate.
Stool occult blood if not pre-menopausal female with heavy menses.
Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Moderate Low). MCV = [value] — workup branches by morphology. Plan: iron studies / B12 / folate as MCV indicates; retic count; peripheral smear; treat underlying cause; recheck in 2–4 weeks.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows moderate anemia. We will check additional tests to find the cause and start treatment. Eat iron-rich foods. Call us if you develop chest pain, shortness of breath with light activity, or feel faint.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Mild Low). MCV = [value]. Plan: workup based on MCV; reproductive-age females — assess menstrual losses; recheck in 4–6 weeks.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows mild anemia. We will look into the cause and may start a supplement. Eat iron-rich foods (lean red meat, beans, leafy greens) with vitamin C to help absorption.

next_tests: Smoking history, sleep apnea screening, SpO2.
If persistent — JAK2 V617F, EPO level for polycythemia vera workup.
Mild High.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Mild High). Plan: hydration assessment; review smoking, sleep apnea symptoms; recheck. If persistent or symptomatic, evaluate for secondary causes vs polycythemia vera.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your red blood cell count is slightly high. This can be related to dehydration, smoking, or sleep issues. We will recheck it and may do additional testing.

Severe High.category: Marked erythrocytosis — concern for polycythemia vera or significant secondary cause
Severe High.ehr_plan: Hgb = {value} g/dL (Severe High). Plan: confirm with repeat; JAK2/EPO; assess for hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, visual changes, plethora, pruritus); hematology referral. Consider therapeutic phlebotomy if Hct >55% or symptomatic.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your red blood cell count is significantly elevated. We will refer you to a blood specialist for further evaluation. Stay well hydrated. Go to the emergency department if you have severe headache, visual changes, chest pain, or signs of a stroke (face droop, arm weakness, speech trouble).

Critical Low.category: Critical thrombocytopenia (<20 K/µL) — spontaneous bleeding risk
next_tests: Repeat CBC w/ peripheral smear (rule out clumping).
HIV, HCV, H. pylori if isolated ITP.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Critical Low). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for hematology evaluation, transfusion if bleeding or platelets <10 K/µL, and workup for ITP, TTP/HUS, DIC, drug-induced, or marrow failure. Strict bleeding precautions: avoid IM injections, NSAIDs, antiplatelets; soft toothbrush; no contact activity.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your platelet count is critically low — there is a high risk of spontaneous bleeding. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Avoid any activity that could cause injury. Do not take aspirin, ibuprofen, or naproxen. If you have any bleeding (nose, gums, urine, stool, severe headache) call 911.

Severe Low.category: Severe thrombocytopenia (20–49 K/µL) — bleeding risk with trauma/procedures
next_tests: Hep B/C, HIV; H. pylori if ITP suspected.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Severe Low). Plan: peripheral smear; coag panel; medication review (hold antiplatelets, NSAIDs); hematology referral; strict bleeding precautions. If active bleeding or rapidly falling — refer to ED.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your platelet count is significantly low. Avoid activities that could cause injury (contact sports, heavy lifting). Do not take aspirin, ibuprofen, or naproxen. Use a soft toothbrush. Call us or go to the emergency department right away if you have any bleeding (nose, gums, easy bruising spreading, blood in urine or stool, severe headache).

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Moderate Low). Plan: peripheral smear; coag panel; review meds (heparin, vancomycin, valproate, sulfa); recheck. Hematology referral if persistent or worsening.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your platelet count is moderately low. We will look into the cause. Avoid heavy contact activity and over-the-counter pain medications like ibuprofen or naproxen. Call us if you develop unusual bruising or bleeding.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Mild Low). Plan: rule out platelet clumping (smear); recheck in 2–4 weeks; medication review.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your platelet count is slightly low. We will recheck it. Often this is not serious, but call us if you notice unusual bruising or bleeding.

next_tests: CRP, ESR (rule out reactive cause: infection, inflammation, iron deficiency).
Mild High.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Mild High). Most often reactive. Plan: workup for inflammation, infection, iron deficiency; recheck in 4–6 weeks.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your platelet count is slightly elevated. This is often related to inflammation, infection, or low iron. We will look into the cause.

next_tests: Consider JAK2 V617F if persistent or signs of myeloproliferative disease.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Moderate High). Plan: rule out reactive causes; if persistent or symptomatic — JAK2 testing and hematology referral.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your platelet count is moderately elevated. We will recheck it and look for a cause.

Severe High.category: Marked thrombocytosis (≥1000 K/µL) — thrombosis / bleeding risk
Severe High.ehr_plan: Platelets = {value} K/µL (Severe High). Plan: hematology referral; assess thrombotic and bleeding risk; consider aspirin if no contraindication. Refer to ED if signs of thrombosis (stroke, MI, DVT/PE).
Severe High.patient_communication: Your platelet count is significantly elevated, which can increase clotting and (paradoxically) bleeding risk. We will refer you to a blood specialist. Go to the emergency department if you have signs of a stroke (face droop, arm weakness, speech trouble), chest pain, leg swelling, or shortness of breath.

wbc.differential_note: ANC = (segs% + bands%) × WBC × 10. ANC <500 = severe neutropenia; ANC <500 + fever (T ≥38.3°C or ≥38°C for >1 h) = febrile neutropenia, oncologic emergency.

Critical Low.category: Critical leukopenia / agranulocytosis (WBC <1.0 K/µL) — severe infection risk
next_tests: Stat CBC w/ differential and ANC.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Critical Low). ANC = [computed]. If febrile or any infection signs — patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for blood cultures and empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (febrile neutropenia is an oncologic emergency). If afebrile — urgent hematology evaluation; reverse isolation precautions; review and hold offending medications.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is critically low — your body cannot fight infections normally. If you have ANY fever (≥100.4°F / 38°C), chills, or signs of infection, go to the nearest emergency department immediately. Avoid crowds, raw foods, and sick contacts. Wash hands frequently. Call us right away if anything changes.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Severe Low). ANC = [computed]. If ANC <500 and febrile — refer to ED. Otherwise: urgent hematology referral; medication review; rule out viral cause (HIV, hepatitis, EBV); CBC trend.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is significantly low. If you develop a fever (≥100.4°F / 38°C), chills, sore throat, or other infection signs, go to the emergency department right away. Wash hands frequently and avoid sick contacts.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Moderate Low). Plan: repeat CBC; review meds; viral workup; recheck within 1–2 weeks; refer hematology if persistent.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is moderately low. We will look into the cause. Call us if you develop fever or signs of infection.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Mild Low). Plan: recheck CBC in 2–4 weeks; medication review; benign ethnic neutropenia possible (especially in African ancestry) — assess differential.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is slightly low. We will recheck it. Many people have a mildly low count without any problem.

next_tests: Assess for infection, inflammation, stress, smoking.
Mild High.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Mild High). Most commonly reactive (infection, inflammation, stress, smoking, steroids). Plan: identify and treat underlying cause; recheck after resolution.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is slightly elevated, often a sign of infection or inflammation. We will look for a cause.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Moderate High). Plan: infection workup, treat source if identified; recheck after intervention.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is moderately elevated, suggesting your body is fighting an infection or significant inflammation. We will identify the cause.

Severe High.category: Severe leukocytosis (WBC 25–49.9 K/µL) — concern for serious infection or hematologic disorder
next_tests: Stat CBC w/ differential and smear.
Severe High.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Severe High). Differential and peripheral smear pending. Plan: aggressive infection workup; if hemodynamically unstable or septic appearing — refer to ED. If smear shows blasts or atypical cells — urgent hematology referral.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is significantly elevated. Please come to the clinic today for further evaluation. Go to the emergency department if you have fever with chills, very fast heart rate, low blood pressure symptoms (lightheadedness), severe abdominal or chest pain.

Critical High.category: Critical leukocytosis (≥50 K/µL) — leukemia or leukemoid reaction
next_tests: Stat CBC w/ differential and peripheral smear.
LDH, uric acid, BMP (tumor lysis risk).
Critical High.ehr_plan: WBC = {value} K/µL (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for hematology evaluation, peripheral smear review, and workup for leukemia / leukemoid reaction with attention to leukostasis (especially WBC >100 K/µL with neuro/pulm symptoms).
Critical High.patient_communication: Your white blood cell count is at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself. Watch for confusion, headache, vision changes, or shortness of breath.

alt.guideline_anchor: ACG 2017 (Kwo et al.) for evaluation of abnormal liver chemistries; AASLD 2023 (Rinella et al.) for MASLD/MASH nomenclature and FIB-4–first case-finding.
alt.uln_note: Lab-reported ULN ~33 U/L (women), ~42 U/L (men) — used for the severity bands below. ACG 2017 notes the *true* healthy ULN is lower (29–33 M / 19–25 F); above the healthy range but below lab ULN warrants attention especially with cardiometabolic risk (MASLD case-finding per AASLD 2023).

next_tests: Repeat ALT/AST in 2–4 weeks to confirm.
Compute AST/ALT ratio (>2 suggests alcoholic pattern; <1 typical of viral / MASLD).
Hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs) and Hepatitis C (anti-HCV, with HCV RNA if positive).
Cardiometabolic risk for MASLD case-finding (per AASLD 2023): BMI, A1C, lipid panel, BP.
Medication review (acetaminophen, statins, NSAIDs, supplements) and alcohol screen (AUDIT-C).
If persistent ≥6 months OR cardiometabolic risk: FIB-4 score (age, AST, ALT, platelets); RUQ ultrasound.
Second-tier workup if persistent: ferritin + transferrin saturation (hemochromatosis); ANA / ASMA / IgG (autoimmune hepatitis); ceruloplasmin if age <40 (Wilson's); alpha-1 antitrypsin level.
Mild High.ehr_plan: ALT = {value} U/L (Mild High; <3× lab ULN). Plan: medication and alcohol review; HBV/HCV serology; cardiometabolic workup for MASLD case-finding (BMI, A1C, lipids); recheck ALT/AST in 2–4 weeks. If persistent ≥6 months or cardiometabolic risk — compute FIB-4, RUQ ultrasound; second-tier workup (iron studies, ANA/ASMA/IgG, ceruloplasmin if <40, A1AT).
Mild High.patient_communication: Your liver enzyme is mildly elevated. The most common causes are medication, alcohol, viral hepatitis, or fatty liver disease (now called MASLD — metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease). We will check additional tests and recheck the level in 2–4 weeks. Avoid alcohol; tell us about all medications and supplements (including over-the-counter); aim for healthy weight, blood pressure, and blood sugar.

next_tests: Full hepatic panel: total/direct bilirubin, ALP, GGT, albumin, PT/INR.
Viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, anti-HCV, HCV RNA).
Acetaminophen level if any ingestion possible.
Iron studies (hemochromatosis); ceruloplasmin if age <40 (Wilson's); A1AT level.
Cardiometabolic workup (A1C, lipids, BMI) and FIB-4 score for MASLD risk stratification.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: ALT = {value} U/L (Moderate High; 3–10× ULN). Plan: full hepatic panel including synthetic function; viral hepatitis panel; RUQ ultrasound; second-tier workup (iron studies, autoimmune, ceruloplasmin if <40, A1AT); FIB-4 score and MASLD case-finding; medication and alcohol review; hepatology referral if persistent or synthetic dysfunction (INR >1.5, low albumin, hypoglycemia, encephalopathy).
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your liver enzymes are moderately elevated. We will check additional tests including for hepatitis, fatty liver disease (MASLD), and other causes. Avoid alcohol completely. Stop or limit acetaminophen (Tylenol). Tell us about all medications and supplements. Go to the emergency department if you develop yellow skin or eyes, severe abdominal pain, confusion, or unusual bleeding.

Severe High.category: Severe ALT elevation (>10× ULN) — possible acute hepatitis
next_tests: Stat hepatic panel: total/direct bilirubin, ALP, GGT, albumin, PT/INR.
Acute hepatitis workup including HSV, CMV, EBV if immunocompromised.
Severe High.ehr_plan: ALT = {value} U/L (Severe High; >10× ULN) — concern for acute hepatitis (drug-induced, viral, ischemic, autoimmune). PT/INR, bilirubin, encephalopathy assessment. If any sign of acute liver failure (encephalopathy, INR >1.5) — patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for transplant-center referral. Otherwise — urgent hepatology consultation within 24–48 h.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your liver enzymes are very significantly elevated, which can mean acute hepatitis. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department right away if you have yellow skin or eyes, confusion, severe nausea or vomiting, abdominal pain, or any unusual bleeding or bruising. Stop alcohol and any non-essential medications including acetaminophen until reviewed.

Critical High.category: Critical ALT elevation (>1000 U/L) — possible acute liver failure
Critical High.ehr_plan: ALT = {value} U/L (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for evaluation for acute liver failure, transplant-center transfer if encephalopathic or INR >1.5, and treatment of underlying cause (e.g., N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen toxicity).
Critical High.patient_communication: Your liver enzymes are at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself. Tell the ED team about any acetaminophen (Tylenol) use, including in cold/flu medications, and any supplements or alcohol use.

sources: ACG 2017 (Kwo et al.) Evaluation of Abnormal Liver Chemistries
AASLD 2023 MASLD Practice Guidance (Rinella et al.)

ast.guideline_anchor: ACG 2017 (Kwo et al.); AASLD 2023 (Rinella et al.) for MASLD/MASH.
ast.uln_note: ULN ~35 U/L. AST/ALT ratio: >2 suggestive of alcoholic pattern; <1 typical of viral / MASLD pattern.

next_tests: Compute AST/ALT ratio (>2 alcoholic; <1 viral / MASLD).
CK (rule out muscle source if isolated AST elevation).
Cardiometabolic risk for MASLD case-finding (BMI, A1C, lipids, BP).
If persistent: FIB-4, RUQ ultrasound, second-tier workup (iron studies, ANA/ASMA/IgG, ceruloplasmin if <40, A1AT).
Mild High.ehr_plan: AST = {value} U/L (Mild High). AST/ALT ratio = [computed]. Plan: rule out muscle source (CK if isolated AST elevation); HBV/HCV; alcohol/medication review; cardiometabolic workup for MASLD; recheck in 2–4 weeks. If persistent — FIB-4 score, RUQ ultrasound, second-tier workup.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your liver enzyme is mildly elevated. Common causes are medication, alcohol, viral hepatitis, fatty liver disease (MASLD), or muscle injury. We will check related tests and recheck the level. Avoid alcohol; tell us about all medications and supplements.

next_tests: Acetaminophen level if any ingestion possible.
Iron studies, ANA/ASMA/IgG, ceruloplasmin if <40, A1AT.
Cardiometabolic workup and FIB-4 score for MASLD.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: AST = {value} U/L (Moderate High). AST/ALT ratio = [computed]. Plan: full hepatic panel; viral hepatitis serologies; RUQ ultrasound; second-tier workup (iron, autoimmune, ceruloplasmin if <40, A1AT); FIB-4 and MASLD case-finding; alcohol cessation; medication review; consider hepatology referral.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your liver enzymes are moderately elevated. We will check for hepatitis, fatty liver disease (MASLD), and other causes. Avoid alcohol completely. Go to the emergency department if you develop yellow skin or eyes, severe abdominal pain, confusion, or unusual bleeding.

Severe High.category: Severe AST elevation (>10× ULN) — possible acute hepatitis or rhabdomyolysis
Severe High.ehr_plan: AST = {value} U/L (Severe High). AST/ALT ratio = [computed]. Differential includes acute hepatitis vs rhabdomyolysis (check CK). If hepatic — synthetic function (PT/INR, albumin), encephalopathy assessment; if any sign of ALF or rhabdo with AKI — patient instructed to proceed immediately to ED. Otherwise — urgent hepatology or appropriate referral within 24–48 h.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your AST level is very significantly elevated. This may indicate liver inflammation or muscle injury. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department if you have yellow skin or eyes, confusion, dark cola-colored urine, severe muscle pain, or unusual bleeding.

Critical High.category: Critical AST elevation (>1000 U/L) — possible acute liver failure or rhabdomyolysis
Critical High.ehr_plan: AST = {value} U/L (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for evaluation of acute liver failure or rhabdomyolysis with AKI risk; transplant-center transfer if hepatic ALF.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your AST level is at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself. Tell the ED team about any acetaminophen use, supplements, alcohol use, recent intense exercise, falls, or muscle injury.

sources: AASLD 2023 MASLD Practice Guidance (Rinella et al.)

bicarbonate.anion_gap_note: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3); normal 8–12. Engine computes this in evaluate_panel when Na, Cl, and HCO3 are all in the input set; UI surfaces it in the session-derived block.

next_tests: Salicylate level if intentional ingestion suspected.
Critical Low.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Critical Low). Anion gap = [computed]. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for ABG, lactate, workup of severe metabolic acidosis (DKA, lactic acidosis, AKI/uremia, toxic alcohol/salicylate ingestion), and IV bicarbonate / definitive treatment.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows your body is severely acidic. This is a medical emergency. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Severe Low). Anion gap = [computed]. Plan: workup for cause (DKA, lactic acidosis, uremia, ingestion); refer to ED for monitored treatment.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows significant acidity. Please go to the emergency department for evaluation and treatment.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Moderate Low). Anion gap = [computed]. Plan: identify cause; if anion gap acidosis with DKA, lactic acidosis, or AKI — refer for inpatient management. If non-anion gap (RTA, GI losses) — workup and outpatient management possible.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your blood test shows moderate acidity. We will check additional tests to find the cause. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department if you feel very weak, are vomiting, have rapid breathing, or feel confused.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Mild Low). Anion gap = [computed]. Plan: identify cause (mild dehydration, GI losses, early CKD, mild DKA); recheck.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your bicarbonate is slightly low, suggesting mild acidity in your blood. We will look into the cause.

Mild High.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Mild High). K = [value]. Plan: assess for vomiting, NG suction, diuretic use; replete K and Cl as needed; recheck.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your bicarbonate level is slightly elevated. We will look into the cause and may adjust your medications.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Moderate High). Plan: workup with urine Cl (saline-responsive vs unresponsive); replete K, Mg; treat underlying cause.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your bicarbonate is moderately elevated. Please come to the clinic for further evaluation.

Severe High.category: Severe metabolic alkalosis (HCO3 ≥40 mEq/L)
Severe High.ehr_plan: HCO3 = {value} mEq/L (Severe High). Plan: refer to ED for monitored correction (especially if symptomatic with weakness, arrhythmia, or seizure); identify and treat cause.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your bicarbonate level is significantly elevated. Please go to the emergency department for evaluation, especially if you feel weak, dizzy, or have abnormal heart rhythms.

Critical Low.category: Critical hypomagnesemia (<1.0 mg/dL) — arrhythmia / seizure risk
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Critical Low). ECG obtained — [QT, findings]. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV magnesium replacement and continuous cardiac monitoring; replete K and Ca which often co-exist.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is critically low. This can cause dangerous heart rhythm problems and seizures. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low). ECG obtained — [findings]. Plan: oral or IV Mg repletion based on symptoms; check and replete K/Ca; review meds (PPI, diuretic, alcohol). If symptomatic (tetany, seizure, arrhythmia) — refer to ED.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is significantly low. We will start a magnesium supplement. Go to the emergency department if you have muscle cramps, twitching, palpitations, or seizures.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Mild Low). Plan: oral Mg repletion (e.g., magnesium oxide 400 mg daily); review meds; recheck in 1–2 weeks.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is slightly low. We will start a supplement. Eat magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, leafy greens, whole grains).

Mild High.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan: hold Mg-containing products; assess renal function; recheck.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is slightly high. Avoid magnesium-containing antacids and laxatives. We will recheck the level.

Severe High.category: Severe hypermagnesemia (3.5–4.9 mg/dL) — neuromuscular/cardiac depression
Severe High.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). Plan: refer to ED for IV calcium gluconate, supportive care, and consideration of dialysis if severe AKI; hold all Mg-containing medications/products.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is significantly elevated. Please go to the emergency department. Stop any magnesium products.

Critical High.ehr_plan: Mg = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV calcium, dialysis, and cardiopulmonary support.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your magnesium level is at a life-threatening level. Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency department right now.

Critical Low.category: Critical hypophosphatemia (<1.0 mg/dL) — risk of respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, hemolysis
next_tests: Review for refeeding syndrome (recent malnutrition + carbohydrate load).
Critical Low.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Critical Low). Concern for refeeding syndrome or severe depletion. Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for IV phosphate replacement and monitoring; review nutritional status.
Critical Low.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is critically low. Please go to the emergency department right now.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low). Plan: oral or IV phosphate replacement; check Mg and K; review for refeeding syndrome, alcohol use; recheck within days.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is significantly low. Please come to the clinic today for further evaluation. We may start a supplement.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Moderate Low). Plan: oral phosphate repletion; vitamin D level; recheck.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is moderately low. We will look into the cause and may start a supplement.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Mild Low). Plan: dietary repletion; recheck.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your phosphate is slightly low. Eat phosphate-rich foods (dairy, nuts, beans, fish).

Mild High.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan: assess renal function; review supplements/laxatives; recheck.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is slightly high. Often this is related to kidney function or diet.

next_tests: Consider tumor lysis labs if oncology context.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Plan: workup for CKD, hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D toxicity, tumor lysis. Phosphate binder consideration if CKD.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is moderately elevated. We will check kidney function and other tests.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperphosphatemia (≥7.0 mg/dL) — concern for tumor lysis or AKI
Severe High.ehr_plan: Phos = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). Plan: refer to ED — concern for tumor lysis syndrome, AKI, or massive cellular release; calcium binders, IV hydration, possible dialysis.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your phosphate level is significantly elevated. Please go to the emergency department for evaluation.

albumin.calcium_correction_use: Used in Ca correction formula: corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 − albumin).

next_tests: Stool studies if GI losses suspected.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: Albumin = {value} g/dL (Severe Low). Plan: workup for cause — chronic liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, protein-losing enteropathy, severe malnutrition, capillary leak. Synthetic function assessment (PT/INR); urine studies; address underlying cause.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your albumin level is significantly low. We will look into the cause, which can include kidney, liver, or nutritional issues. Please come to the clinic for further evaluation.

Moderate Low.ehr_plan: Albumin = {value} g/dL (Moderate Low). Plan: workup as above; nutritional optimization; treat underlying cause.
Moderate Low.patient_communication: Your albumin level is moderately low. We will look into the cause.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: Albumin = {value} g/dL (Mild Low). Plan: assess for inflammation, mild malnutrition, early renal/hepatic disease; recheck.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your albumin level is slightly low. We will look into the cause and recheck the level.

Mild High.ehr_plan: Albumin = {value} g/dL (Mild High). Most often relative — assess hydration; recheck after rehydration.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your albumin is slightly high, often from dehydration. Drink fluids and we will recheck.

mcv.interpretation_note: MCV directs anemia workup: <80 microcytic; 80–100 normocytic; >100 macrocytic. Always interpret with hemoglobin.

Severe Low.category: Severe microcytosis (MCV <70 fL) — concerning for thalassemia
next_tests: Iron studies (Fe, TIBC, ferritin, transferrin sat).
Severe Low.ehr_plan: MCV = {value} fL (Severe Low). Plan: iron studies + Hgb electrophoresis; consider thalassemia trait, severe iron deficiency, or anemia of chronic disease.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your red blood cells are unusually small. We will check iron levels and a hemoglobin test to find the cause.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: MCV = {value} fL (Mild Low). Plan: iron studies; treat iron deficiency if confirmed; consider thalassemia trait.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your red blood cells are smaller than normal, often from iron deficiency. We will check your iron levels.

Mild High.ehr_plan: MCV = {value} fL (Mild High). Plan: B12/folate, TSH, LFTs; alcohol review; medication review (hydroxyurea, methotrexate, AZT, certain anticonvulsants).
Mild High.patient_communication: Your red blood cells are larger than normal. Common causes include B12 or folate deficiency, thyroid disease, or alcohol use. We will check additional tests.

Severe High.category: Severe macrocytosis (MCV ≥110 fL) — concern for B12/folate deficiency or MDS
next_tests: If unexplained — hematology referral for MDS workup.
Severe High.ehr_plan: MCV = {value} fL (Severe High). Plan: full macrocytic workup; if B12 <200 pg/mL — replete IM; hematology referral if unexplained or smear abnormal.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your red blood cells are significantly larger than normal. We will check several tests to find the cause and start treatment.

alkaline_phosphatase.context_note: ALP elevation may be hepatic (cholestatic) or bony — check GGT to localize: GGT elevated → hepatic; GGT normal → bone or other. Pregnancy and growing children/adolescents have physiologically elevated ALP. Engine selects pregnancy bands when context.pregnancy is True (placental ALP raises ALP 2–3× baseline, especially T3).

Mild High.ehr_plan: ALP = {value} U/L (Mild High). Plan: GGT; hepatic panel; if pregnant or adolescent, may be physiologic; recheck in 4–6 weeks.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your alkaline phosphatase is slightly elevated. We will check additional tests to determine if this is from your liver, bones, or another source.

next_tests: Bone-specific workup if GGT normal: vitamin D, calcium, PTH, consider bone scan.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: ALP = {value} U/L (Moderate High). GGT [pending/value]. If hepatic pattern (GGT elevated) — RUQ ultrasound, hepatic workup. If bony — vitamin D, Ca, PTH, consider bone disease (Paget's, osteomalacia, mets).
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your alkaline phosphatase is moderately elevated. We will check additional tests to find the source.

Severe High.category: Severe ALP elevation (>5× ULN) — significant cholestasis or bone disease
next_tests: If bony — bone scan, oncologic workup.
Severe High.ehr_plan: ALP = {value} U/L (Severe High). Plan: full hepatic workup with imaging; if bony — oncologic workup including bone scan and tumor markers. Hepatology or oncology referral as indicated.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your alkaline phosphatase is significantly elevated. We will work up the cause carefully. Go to the emergency department if you develop yellow skin or eyes, severe abdominal pain, fever with chills, or unusual bleeding.

total_bilirubin.fractionation_note: Direct bilirubin >0.4 mg/dL or >20% of total suggests conjugated/cholestatic pattern. Indirect/unconjugated suggests hemolysis or Gilbert's.

next_tests: If unconjugated and otherwise normal — consider Gilbert's syndrome.
Mild High.ehr_plan: TBili = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Direct/indirect: [pending]. Plan: fractionation; if indirect predominant — hemolysis workup or Gilbert's; if direct — hepatic workup.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your bilirubin is mildly elevated. We will check additional tests to determine the cause.

Moderate High.category: Moderate hyperbilirubinemia (3.0–9.9 mg/dL) — clinical jaundice usually present
Moderate High.ehr_plan: TBili = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Direct/indirect: [pending]. Plan: imaging if cholestatic; viral hepatitis workup; hemolysis workup if indirect; hepatology referral if persistent or worsening.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your bilirubin is moderately elevated and may be causing yellow color in your skin or eyes. We will check additional tests including an ultrasound. Go to the emergency department if you develop severe abdominal pain, fever with chills, confusion, or unusual bleeding.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperbilirubinemia (10.0–19.9 mg/dL) — significant hepatic dysfunction or biliary obstruction
Severe High.ehr_plan: TBili = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). Plan: workup for cause; assess for acute liver failure (encephalopathy, INR >1.5) — if present, refer to ED for transplant-center evaluation. Otherwise — urgent hepatology / GI consultation.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your bilirubin is significantly elevated. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department if you have confusion, severe abdominal pain, fever, or unusual bleeding.

Critical High.category: Critical hyperbilirubinemia (≥20 mg/dL) — likely advanced ALF or biliary obstruction
Critical High.ehr_plan: TBili = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for evaluation of acute liver failure or major biliary obstruction; transplant-center referral if ALF.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your bilirubin is at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

Mild High.ehr_plan: A1C = {value}% (Prediabetes). Plan: lifestyle counseling — diet (Mediterranean / low-glycemic), ≥150 min/week activity, weight management; consider metformin per ADA for high-risk (BMI ≥35, age <60, prior GDM); recheck A1C in 6 months.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your A1C is in the prediabetes range. Lifestyle changes are very effective at preventing diabetes. We will discuss diet, activity, and weight management, and recheck the test in 6 months.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: A1C = {value}% (Diabetes range). Plan: if new diagnosis — diabetes education, baseline labs (lipids, microalbumin), eye and foot exam referrals, lifestyle counseling, initiate metformin per ADA. If established diabetes — review and adjust regimen toward individualized target (commonly <7%).
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your A1C is in the diabetes range. We will work together on a treatment plan including diet, activity, and medication. We will also schedule eye and foot exams to protect against complications.

Severe High.category: Poorly controlled diabetes (A1C 8.0–11.9%)
Severe High.ehr_plan: A1C = {value}% (Severe High). Plan: intensify regimen per ADA Standards; assess for medication adherence, lifestyle barriers; consider GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i (cardiorenal benefit); endocrinology referral if escalation difficult; complications screening.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your A1C is significantly elevated, meaning your blood sugar has been high for several months. We will adjust your treatment plan together. Watch for increased thirst, urination, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds, or unusual fatigue — call us if these worsen.

Critical High.ehr_plan: A1C = {value}% (Critical High). Plan: aggressive regimen intensification — likely insulin if not already; rule out DKA (ketones, anion gap) especially in T1D; endocrinology referral; close follow-up; consider hospitalization if symptomatic, ketotic, or unable to manage outpatient.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your A1C is at a critical level. Please come to the clinic this week for a comprehensive plan. Go to the emergency department if you have nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, or feel confused — these can be signs of a diabetic emergency.

tsh.context_note: TSH reference is age- and pregnancy-dependent. Pregnancy bands per ATA 2017 (Alexander et al., Thyroid) — trimester-specific bands selected when context.trimester ∈ {1,2,3}; generic pregnancy fallback otherwise. Elderly band (age ≥70) widens the upper limit to 5.0 mIU/L per ATA hypothyroidism guidance on age-related TSH rise. Always interpret with free T4.

Severe Low.category: Suppressed TSH (<0.1 mIU/L) — likely overt hyperthyroidism
next_tests: Thyroid uptake scan if etiology unclear.
Severe Low.ehr_plan: TSH = {value} mIU/L (Severe Low). Plan: free T4, free T3; assess for Graves' (TRAb), toxic nodule, thyroiditis; if symptomatic (tachycardia, weight loss, tremor, AFib) — beta-blocker; endocrinology referral. If thyroid storm features (hyperthermia, AFib with RVR, altered mentation) — refer to ED.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your thyroid test suggests an overactive thyroid. We will check additional tests and refer you to a specialist. Go to the emergency department if you have very rapid heart rate, high fever, severe agitation, or confusion.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: TSH = {value} mIU/L (Mild Low). Plan: free T4/T3; recheck in 6–12 weeks; treatment depends on T4, age, comorbidities (atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis risk).
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your thyroid test is slightly low. We will check additional tests and recheck the level.

Mild High.ehr_plan: TSH = {value} mIU/L (Mild High). Plan: free T4; TPO antibodies; recheck. Treatment if symptomatic, TPO+, pregnant/planning pregnancy, or persistent TSH >10.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your thyroid test is slightly high, suggesting a mildly underactive thyroid. We will check additional tests and decide whether treatment is needed.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: TSH = {value} mIU/L (Moderate High). Plan: initiate levothyroxine (typical starting dose ~1.6 mcg/kg, lower in elderly or cardiac disease); recheck TSH in 6–8 weeks.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your thyroid is underactive. We will start a thyroid hormone replacement medication. We will recheck the level in 6–8 weeks to adjust the dose.

Severe High.category: Severe hypothyroidism (TSH ≥20 mIU/L) — myxedema risk
next_tests: If altered mental status, hypothermia, hypotension — myxedema coma workup.
Severe High.ehr_plan: TSH = {value} mIU/L (Severe High). Plan: initiate levothyroxine; close follow-up. If altered mental status, hypothermia, bradycardia, or hyponatremia — refer to ED for evaluation of myxedema coma (IV levothyroxine + hydrocortisone).
Severe High.patient_communication: Your thyroid test shows significantly underactive thyroid. We will start medication. Go to the emergency department if you feel very cold, very sleepy or confused, or have a slow heart rate or trouble breathing.

sources: ATA 2017 pregnancy guidelines (Alexander et al., Thyroid 2017)

bun.ratio_note: BUN/Cr ratio: >20 suggests prerenal azotemia (volume depletion, GI bleed) or high protein load; <10 suggests intrinsic renal disease, malnutrition, or liver disease. Engine computes BUN/Cr in evaluate_panel when both are present and surfaces the prerenal/intrinsic interpretation under both labs.

Mild High.ehr_plan: BUN = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Cr = [value], BUN/Cr = [computed]. Plan: assess hydration, GI bleeding history, protein load, renal function.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your BUN is slightly elevated, often related to dehydration. Drink water normally. We will compare with kidney function.

next_tests: Stool occult blood if GI bleed suspected.
Moderate High.ehr_plan: BUN = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Plan: assess for AKI vs CKD (compare to Cr and baseline); rule out GI bleed; hydration status; recheck.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your BUN is moderately elevated. We will check kidney function and look for the cause.

Severe High.category: Severe BUN elevation — significant uremia
Severe High.ehr_plan: BUN = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). Plan: refer to ED if symptomatic (uremia symptoms, AKI signs, hyperkalemia, acidosis); evaluate for GI bleed and AKI.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your BUN is significantly elevated. Please come to the clinic today, or go to the emergency department if you feel confused, very weak, have decreased urination, swelling, shortness of breath, or any GI bleeding.

Critical High.category: Critical BUN elevation — uremia / RRT consideration
Critical High.ehr_plan: BUN = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department for AKI workup, electrolyte management, and possible renal replacement therapy.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your BUN is at a critical level. Please go to the nearest emergency department right now. Do not drive yourself.

chloride.context_note: Chloride usually parallels sodium. Main interpretive value is in anion gap calculation (Na − Cl − HCO3). Standalone chloride abnormalities rarely trigger acute action.

next_tests: BMP (Na, HCO3 — assess for metabolic alkalosis pattern).
Mild Low.ehr_plan: Cl = {value} mEq/L (Mild Low). Often accompanies metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics) or hyponatremia. Address underlying cause.
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your chloride is slightly low. We will look at your other electrolyte levels to find the cause.

Severe Low.ehr_plan: Cl = {value} mEq/L (Severe Low). Likely metabolic alkalosis or significant fluid loss. Plan: evaluate cause; saline resuscitation if volume-depleted; treat underlying.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your chloride is significantly low. Please come to the clinic for further evaluation.

Mild High.ehr_plan: Cl = {value} mEq/L (Mild High). Often accompanies non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (RTA, GI losses, saline). Address underlying cause.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your chloride is slightly elevated. We will look at your other electrolytes.

Severe High.category: Severe hyperchloremia
Severe High.ehr_plan: Cl = {value} mEq/L (Severe High). Plan: workup for non-anion-gap acidosis; address cause; recheck.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your chloride is significantly elevated. Please come to the clinic for evaluation.

total_cholesterol.panel_note: Total cholesterol is one component of the lipid panel; LDL is the primary actionable target. PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk is computed automatically when sufficient cardiovascular inputs are available (sidebar).

next_tests: Confirm full lipid panel: LDL, HDL, triglycerides, non-HDL.
Compute PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk (see derived values panel).
A1C, BMP/eGFR, BMI, BP if not recent.
Mild High.ehr_plan: Total cholesterol = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan: assess full lipid panel (LDL is the primary target); PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk for statin decision per 2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline; lifestyle counseling.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your total cholesterol is slightly above normal. We will look at your LDL ('bad' cholesterol), HDL ('good' cholesterol), and triglycerides individually to decide on the best treatment. Lifestyle changes — heart-healthy diet, regular activity, weight management — help most people.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: Total cholesterol = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Plan: full lipid panel; PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk for statin decision; lifestyle counseling.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your total cholesterol is high. We will check your LDL, HDL, and triglyceride numbers and use them with your other risk factors to decide whether you need a statin medication. Lifestyle changes are part of the plan either way.

Severe High.category: Markedly elevated total cholesterol (≥290 mg/dL) — consider familial hypercholesterolemia
next_tests: Full lipid panel with LDL (FH workup if LDL ≥190).
TSH (rule out hypothyroidism); UA (nephrotic syndrome); fasting glucose/A1C; LFTs.
Severe High.ehr_plan: Total cholesterol = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). LDL likely ≥190 — concern for familial hypercholesterolemia. Plan: high-intensity statin regardless of computed PREVENT risk; family screening; consider lipid-specialist referral; rule out secondary causes (hypothyroid, nephrotic, uncontrolled DM, cholestatic liver disease).
Severe High.patient_communication: Your total cholesterol is very high. We will likely start a strong cholesterol-lowering medication (statin) and check whether this runs in your family. We will also screen for other causes such as thyroid disease.

ldl_cholesterol.panel_note: LDL is the primary actionable target. PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk drives statin intensity per 2026 ACC/AHA guideline.

next_tests: 30-yr ASCVD risk if age 30–59 (consider for younger pts).
Cardiometabolic baseline (A1C, BMP, BP, BMI, urine ACR if diabetic).
Mild High.ehr_plan: LDL = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan per 2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline: statin decision driven by PREVENT 10-yr ASCVD risk — ≥10% high-intensity, 3–10% moderate-intensity, <3% lifestyle (consider statin if 30-yr risk ≥10%). Lifestyle counseling.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your LDL ('bad' cholesterol) is slightly above normal. We will use a calculator (PREVENT) along with your blood pressure, smoking status, weight, and other factors to decide whether starting a statin medication makes sense. Lifestyle changes — heart-healthy diet, regular activity, weight management — help regardless.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: LDL = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Per 2026 ACC/AHA guideline: moderate-intensity statin reasonable in most adults with LDL 160–189 even at low PREVENT risk if 30-yr risk ≥10%; high-intensity statin if PREVENT 10-yr ≥10%. Lifestyle counseling. Rule out secondary causes (hypothyroid, nephrotic).
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your LDL is moderately high. Most people with this level benefit from a statin medication. We will look at your overall heart-disease risk to choose the dose. Lifestyle changes are part of the plan.

Severe High.category: Severely elevated LDL (190–249 mg/dL) — likely familial hypercholesterolemia
next_tests: High-intensity statin (regardless of PREVENT risk per 2026 ACC/AHA).
Family screening for FH (cascade screening of first-degree relatives).
TSH, UA, fasting glucose, LFTs (rule out secondary).
Severe High.ehr_plan: LDL = {value} mg/dL (Severe High) — clinical FH likely. Plan: initiate high-intensity statin regardless of PREVENT 10-yr risk per 2026 ACC/AHA guideline; obtain Lp(a); cascade screening of first-degree relatives; rule out secondary causes; consider lipid specialist / genetics referral.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your LDL is very high — at a level that often runs in families (familial hypercholesterolemia). We will start a strong statin medication and check whether your close family members should be tested too. This is treatable and important to address early.

Critical High.category: Critically elevated LDL (≥250 mg/dL) — severe / homozygous FH consideration
next_tests: High-intensity statin + ezetimibe; consider PCSK9 inhibitor.
Lipidology referral (urgent if homozygous FH suspected, especially if pediatric).
Consider apheresis center if LDL not controlled.
Critical High.ehr_plan: LDL = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Concern for severe heterozygous or homozygous FH. Plan: aggressive lipid-lowering — high-intensity statin + ezetimibe, consider PCSK9 inhibitor; urgent lipidology referral; genetic testing and family cascade screening; rule out secondary causes.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your LDL is at a critical level. This is a serious form of familial high cholesterol that needs specialist care. We will start medications now and refer you to a lipid specialist. Your close family members should also be tested.

hdl_cholesterol.context_note: HDL low-thresholds are sex-stratified: <40 men, <50 women per ATP-III. HDL ≥60 is a 'negative risk factor.' HDL itself is not a treatment target; intervention focuses on LDL and PREVENT-driven risk.

next_tests: Full lipid panel (often paired with elevated TG).
Severe Low.ehr_plan: HDL = {value} mg/dL (Severe Low). Often a marker of metabolic syndrome.
