Plan: address LDL and overall cardiometabolic risk via PREVENT-driven decisions; aerobic exercise, weight loss, smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet improve HDL modestly; HDL is not a direct treatment target.
Severe Low.patient_communication: Your HDL ('good' cholesterol) is very low. The most effective steps are regular aerobic exercise, weight management, not smoking, and a heart-healthy diet. We will focus on your overall heart-disease risk and your LDL.

Mild Low.ehr_plan: HDL = {value} mg/dL (Mild Low for sex). Plan: address LDL and overall cardiometabolic risk via PREVENT; lifestyle (exercise, weight, no smoking, Mediterranean-style diet).
Mild Low.patient_communication: Your HDL ('good' cholesterol) is on the low side. Aerobic exercise, weight management, not smoking, and a heart-healthy diet help raise it. We will focus mainly on your overall heart-disease risk.

Mild High.category: High HDL (≥60 mg/dL) — negative risk factor
next_tests: Full lipid panel for LDL focus.
Mild High.ehr_plan: HDL = {value} mg/dL (Mild High; ≥60 is a 'negative risk factor' per ATP-III). No action required for HDL itself; focus on LDL and PREVENT-driven decisions.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your HDL ('good' cholesterol) is high — that is favorable for your heart. No action needed for this number; we will still look at your LDL and other risk factors.

triglycerides.fasting_note: Fasting status affects interpretation; non-fasting TG ≥175 is abnormal per 2026 guideline.

Mild High.ehr_plan: TG = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan: lifestyle (weight, exercise, low-glycemic diet, alcohol reduction); rule out secondary causes (DM, hypothyroid, alcohol, meds); PREVENT-driven statin decision based on overall risk.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your triglycerides are slightly high. Limit added sugars, refined carbs, and alcohol; focus on regular exercise and weight management. We will check related tests.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: TG = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Plan: address LDL/non-HDL primarily (statin); intensive lifestyle; rule out secondary causes (uncontrolled DM, hypothyroid, alcohol, meds); REDUCE-IT criteria — consider icosapent ethyl if TG 150–499 with established ASCVD or DM + ≥1 risk factor on max-tolerated statin.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your triglycerides are moderately high. We will focus on your LDL with a possible statin and on lifestyle (low-sugar/low-refined-carb diet, weight management, limit alcohol). We will also check thyroid and diabetes labs.

Severe High.category: Severe hypertriglyceridemia (500–999 mg/dL) — pancreatitis risk
Severe High.ehr_plan: TG = {value} mg/dL (Severe High) — increased pancreatitis risk. Plan: fibrate (fenofibrate) and/or icosapent ethyl; tight glycemic control if diabetic; alcohol cessation; very-low-fat diet; high-intensity statin per ASCVD risk. If abdominal pain — refer to ED for pancreatitis workup.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your triglycerides are very high — high enough to put you at risk for pancreatitis (a painful inflammation of the pancreas). We will start medication, focus on diet (very low fat, no alcohol), and treat any contributing conditions. Go to the emergency department right away if you have severe abdominal pain, persistent nausea or vomiting.

Critical High.category: Critical hypertriglyceridemia (≥1000 mg/dL) — pancreatitis emergency risk
next_tests: Calcium (low calcium can occur with pancreatitis).
Critical High.ehr_plan: TG = {value} mg/dL (Critical High). Acute pancreatitis risk is significant. If any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or back pain — patient instructed to proceed immediately to the nearest emergency department. Otherwise: urgent lipid-specialist evaluation, fibrate + icosapent ethyl + insulin if hyperglycemic; very-low-fat diet (≤15% fat); alcohol cessation; consider apheresis if persistently >1000 with symptoms.
Critical High.patient_communication: Your triglycerides are at a level that can cause pancreatitis (a serious inflammation of the pancreas). Please go to the nearest emergency department right away if you have any abdominal or back pain, nausea, or vomiting. Otherwise come to the clinic this week. Stop alcohol completely and start a very low-fat diet. We will start medications urgently.

non_hdl_cholesterol.panel_note: Non-HDL = Total cholesterol − HDL. Captures all atherogenic lipoproteins. Targets are typically LDL goal + 30 mg/dL.

Mild High.ehr_plan: Non-HDL-C = {value} mg/dL (Mild High). Plan: address LDL primarily; PREVENT-driven statin decision; lifestyle counseling.
Mild High.patient_communication: Your non-HDL cholesterol is slightly high. We will focus on your LDL and overall heart-disease risk.

Moderate High.ehr_plan: Non-HDL-C = {value} mg/dL (Moderate High). Plan: statin decision per PREVENT + 2026 ACC/AHA; lifestyle.
Moderate High.patient_communication: Your non-HDL cholesterol is moderately elevated. We will likely start or intensify a statin and continue lifestyle changes.

Severe High.category: Severe non-HDL elevation (≥190 mg/dL) — possible FH or severe combined dyslipidemia
Severe High.ehr_plan: Non-HDL-C = {value} mg/dL (Severe High). Plan: high-intensity statin regardless of PREVENT risk; rule out secondary causes; FH workup if LDL also ≥190; cascade screening; consider lipid specialist.
Severe High.patient_communication: Your non-HDL cholesterol is very high. We will start a strong statin and check whether this runs in your family. We will also look for other causes.

patterns.label: Acute hepatitis B infection
patterns.category: Acute HBV infection — high infectivity, monitor for chronicity
next_tests: ALT, AST, total/direct bilirubin, ALP, albumin, PT/INR (synthetic function).

Full STI panel given shared transmission risk: HIV (4th-gen Ag/Ab), syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HCV (anti-HCV with reflex RNA), gonorrhea + chlamydia NAAT (urine and any exposed site), trichomonas if female.
Discuss with preventive medicine for partner notification, public-health reporting, and exposure / contact tracing per local policy.
Hepatology referral; counsel on infectivity, household / sexual contact testing and post-exposure prophylaxis (HBIG + vaccination per CDC).
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with acute HBV infection (HBsAg+, anti-HBc total+, anti-HBc IgM+, anti-HBs−). Plan: HBV DNA, HBeAg/anti-HBe; LFTs + synthetic function; full STI co-infection screen (HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab, RPR + treponemal, HCV w/ reflex RNA, GC/CT NAAT, trichomonas if female); preventive medicine consult for partner notification + public-health reporting + exposure tracing; counsel on transmission risk; household / sexual contact testing with HBIG + vaccination per CDC; hepatology referral. If signs of acute liver failure (encephalopathy, INR >1.5) — proceed immediately to ED for transplant-center evaluation.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows an acute (recent) hepatitis B infection. Most adults clear this on their own within 6 months, but we need to monitor your liver function and watch for any signs of liver failure. Avoid alcohol completely. Avoid acetaminophen (Tylenol) unless we tell you it is safe. Because hepatitis B can spread through blood and sexual contact, we will also test you for other sexually transmitted infections (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis C, gonorrhea, chlamydia) and connect you with preventive medicine to discuss notifying anyone who may have been exposed. Your household members and sexual partners should be tested and may need vaccination. Please go to the emergency department right away if you develop yellow skin or eyes, severe abdominal pain, confusion, or unusual bleeding.

patterns.label: Chronic hepatitis B infection
patterns.category: Chronic HBV infection — staging + treatment-eligibility workup needed
next_tests: ALT, AST, ALP, total/direct bilirubin, albumin, PT/INR, platelets.
FIB-4 score and / or transient elastography for fibrosis.
Hepatocellular carcinoma screening: AFP + RUQ ultrasound every 6 months for at-risk groups (Asian male ≥40, Asian female ≥50, African ≥20, family history of HCC, cirrhosis).

Full STI co-infection screen given shared transmission risk: HIV (4th-gen Ag/Ab), syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HCV (anti-HCV with reflex RNA), HDV (anti-HDV — co-infection worsens prognosis), gonorrhea + chlamydia NAAT, trichomonas if female.
Discuss with preventive medicine for partner notification and public-health reporting per local policy.
Hepatology referral for treatment-eligibility decision per AASLD.
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with chronic HBV infection (HBsAg+, anti-HBc total+, anti-HBc IgM−, anti-HBs−). Plan: HBV DNA, HBeAg/anti-HBe; LFT panel with synthetic function; FIB-4 / elastography; HCC surveillance per AASLD risk groups; full STI co-infection screen (HIV, RPR + treponemal, HCV, HDV, GC/CT NAAT, trichomonas if female); preventive medicine consult for partner notification and public-health reporting; hepatology referral for treatment-eligibility decision. Counsel on transmission, household / partner testing and vaccination, alcohol avoidance, hepatotoxic medication caution.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows chronic hepatitis B (long-standing infection). Most people with chronic hepatitis B feel well, but we need regular follow-up to check the virus level, your liver, and to screen for liver complications. Because hepatitis B can spread through blood and sexual contact, we will also test you for other sexually transmitted infections (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis C and D, gonorrhea, chlamydia) and connect you with preventive medicine to help notify anyone who may have been exposed. We will refer you to a liver specialist to decide whether antiviral treatment is needed. Avoid alcohol. Tell your doctor about all medications. Your household members and sexual partners should be tested and likely vaccinated.

patterns.label: Acute HBV in window period (HBsAg cleared, anti-HBs not yet developed)
patterns.category: Acute HBV in window period (rare) — between HBsAg clearance and anti-HBs appearance
next_tests: Repeat full panel in 4–6 weeks (anti-HBs should appear).
HBV DNA to confirm clearing infection.
Full STI co-infection screen (active recent infection): HIV (4th-gen Ag/Ab), syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HCV w/ reflex RNA, GC/CT NAAT, trichomonas if female.
Discuss with preventive medicine for partner notification.
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with the window period of acute HBV (anti-HBc IgM+, anti-HBc total+, HBsAg−, anti-HBs−). Plan: repeat panel in 4–6 weeks; HBV DNA; LFTs; full STI co-infection screen (HIV, RPR + treponemal, HCV, GC/CT, trichomonas if female); preventive medicine consult for partner notification; counsel on transmission until anti-HBs appears.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test suggests a recent hepatitis B infection that is in the process of clearing. We will repeat the test in 4–6 weeks to confirm. We will also test for other infections that can spread similarly (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis C, gonorrhea, chlamydia) and connect you with preventive medicine to help notify partners. Until the next test, treat yourself as still potentially infectious — your household members and sexual partners should be tested.

patterns.label: Resolved past HBV infection (immune)
patterns.category: Resolved past HBV infection — natural immunity
next_tests: If immunosuppression planned (chemotherapy, anti-CD20, prolonged steroids, biologics) — HBV DNA + hepatology consult; antiviral prophylaxis to prevent reactivation per AASLD.
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with resolved past HBV infection: natural immunity (HBsAg−, anti-HBs+, anti-HBc total+). No active disease. Counsel on reactivation risk if future immunosuppression — antiviral prophylaxis indicated (per AASLD).
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows you had hepatitis B in the past and your body cleared it. You are now immune. No treatment is needed. If you ever need chemotherapy or strong immune-suppressing medication in the future, tell your doctor — sometimes the virus can reactivate, and a preventive antiviral may be needed.

patterns.label: Immune from vaccination
next_tests: For healthcare workers, document anti-HBs titer ≥10 mIU/mL as evidence of immunity per CDC.
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with vaccine-induced immunity (anti-HBs+, HBsAg−, anti-HBc total−). No infection. Document immune status; no further testing needed.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows you are immune to hepatitis B from vaccination. No follow-up needed.

patterns.label: Isolated anti-HBc (resolved / occult / false-positive)
patterns.category: Isolated anti-HBc — investigate (resolved with anti-HBs lost; occult HBV; or false-positive)
next_tests: Repeat anti-HBc and anti-HBs in 4–6 weeks (rule out lab error / waning immunity).
Full STI screen given shared-risk populations: HIV (4th-gen Ag/Ab), syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HCV (anti-HCV with reflex RNA).
Consider preventive medicine consultation if HBV DNA detectable or other STI screens positive.
If immunosuppression planned — antiviral prophylaxis or close monitoring per AASLD (reactivation risk).
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology shows isolated anti-HBc (HBsAg−, anti-HBs−, anti-HBc total+, anti-HBc IgM−). Plan: HBV DNA to rule out occult infection; repeat panel in 4–6 weeks to confirm; full STI screen (HIV, RPR + treponemal, HCV w/ reflex RNA) given shared-risk overlap; preventive medicine consult if HBV DNA detectable or other STI screens positive; counsel on reactivation risk if future immunosuppression.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows you have been exposed to hepatitis B at some point, but the picture is incomplete. We will check whether the virus is still present at a low level and recheck the antibody levels in 4–6 weeks. Because hepatitis B and other infections can share the same routes of spread, we will also test for HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis C.

patterns.label: Susceptible — no prior exposure or vaccination
next_tests: Hepatitis B vaccine series (3 doses at 0, 1, 6 months; or 2-dose Heplisav-B).
Document anti-HBs titer 1–2 months after the final dose for healthcare workers and high-risk groups.
patterns.ehr_plan: Hep B serology consistent with susceptibility (all markers negative). Plan: vaccinate with HBV series (3-dose recombinant or 2-dose Heplisav-B); document anti-HBs ≥10 mIU/mL post-series for healthcare workers and high-risk groups per CDC.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows you have never had hepatitis B and are not vaccinated. We recommend the hepatitis B vaccine series — it is safe and very effective. We will start the first dose today.

fallback.label: Indeterminate / atypical pattern — repeat
fallback.category: Atypical Hep B serology pattern — repeat to rule out lab error
next_tests: Repeat full Hep B serology panel in 4–6 weeks.
fallback.ehr_plan: Hep B serology shows an atypical pattern. Plan: repeat panel in 4–6 weeks (rule out lab error / transition); HBV DNA if clinically suspicious; hepatology if pattern persists.
fallback.patient_communication: Your hepatitis B blood test result is unusual and may need to be repeated. We will recheck in 4–6 weeks.

sources: CDC: Interpretation of Hepatitis B Serologic Test Results
AASLD 2018 Hepatitis B Guidance (Terrault et al.)

patterns.label: HIV non-reactive — no infection (or pre-window-period)
patterns.category: HIV screen non-reactive — no laboratory evidence of infection
next_tests: If recent high-risk exposure (within 4 weeks): repeat 4th-gen Ag/Ab in 4–6 weeks (window period coverage); offer PrEP per CDC.
If ongoing risk: re-screen at recommended interval (every 3–6 months for high-risk; annually for moderate risk).
Continue full STI screen (syphilis RPR + treponemal, HCV, HBV serology, GC/CT NAAT all-site, trichomonas) — HIV negative does not rule out other STIs.
patterns.ehr_plan: HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab screen non-reactive. No laboratory evidence of HIV infection. Plan: if recent (<4 wk) high-risk exposure — repeat in 4–6 weeks for window-period coverage; offer PrEP per CDC if ongoing risk; complete full STI panel (RPR + treponemal, HCV, HBV, GC/CT NAAT, trichomonas).
patterns.patient_communication: Your HIV test is negative. If you may have been exposed in the last few weeks, the test can sometimes miss very recent infection — we will repeat it in 4 to 6 weeks. We can also discuss PrEP, a daily pill that prevents HIV. Please complete the rest of the STI testing today.

patterns.label: HIV infection confirmed (HIV-1 or HIV-2)
patterns.category: Confirmed HIV infection — link to care urgently
next_tests: HIV-1 viral load (RNA quantitative) and CD4 count — baseline staging.
HIV genotype (resistance testing) before initiating ART.
CMP, CBC, LFTs, lipid panel, fasting glucose / A1C, urinalysis (baseline metabolic + renal + hepatic).

Full STI co-infection screen: syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HBV serology (HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc total), HCV (anti-HCV with reflex RNA), GC + CT NAAT (all exposed sites including pharyngeal and rectal), trichomonas if female.
Latent TB infection: IGRA (preferred) or PPD; chest X-ray if positive.
Toxoplasma IgG, CMV IgG, VZV IgG, measles immunity (IgG) — baseline.
Vaccinations: review HBV / HAV / HPV / pneumococcal / influenza / COVID / Tdap status.
Discuss with preventive medicine for partner notification, public-health reporting (HIV is reportable in all states), and contact tracing per local policy.
Refer to HIV-specialty care within 1 week — rapid ART initiation per DHHS / IAS-USA.
patterns.ehr_plan: HIV confirmed — Ag/Ab screen reactive AND HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation reactive. Plan: HIV-1 viral load + CD4 baseline; resistance genotype; CMP / CBC / LFTs / lipids / glucose / UA; full STI co-infection screen (RPR + treponemal, HBV serology, HCV w/ reflex RNA, GC/CT NAAT all-site, trichomonas if female); latent TB (IGRA preferred); toxo / CMV / VZV / measles IgG; pregnancy test if applicable; review and update HBV/HAV/HPV/pneumococcal/influenza/COVID/Tdap vaccinations; preventive medicine consult for partner notification + reporting; HIV-specialty referral within 1 week for rapid ART initiation.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test confirms HIV infection. With modern treatment, HIV is a manageable chronic condition and people on consistent therapy can have a normal life expectancy and cannot transmit the virus to partners (Undetectable = Untransmittable). We will refer you urgently to an HIV specialist to start medication, ideally within a week. We will also test for other infections that share the same routes of spread (syphilis, hepatitis B and C, gonorrhea, chlamydia, trichomonas) and check for latent tuberculosis. A preventive medicine team will help with notifying anyone who may have been exposed — this is confidential and required by public health law. Please take care of yourself; we are here to support you.

patterns.label: Acute HIV-1 infection (antibody-negative window)
patterns.category: Acute HIV-1 infection — high transmissibility, urgent linkage to care
next_tests: HIV-1 viral load (quantitative) and CD4 — acute infection often has very high viral load.
CMP, CBC, LFTs, lipid panel, fasting glucose / A1C, urinalysis.
Full STI co-infection screen: syphilis (RPR + treponemal), HBV serology, HCV (anti-HCV + RNA), GC + CT NAAT all exposed sites, trichomonas if female.
Latent TB (IGRA preferred); chest X-ray if symptomatic or IGRA positive.
Discuss with preventive medicine immediately — acute HIV is reportable, contacts may benefit from PEP (within 72 h of exposure).
Refer to HIV-specialty care within 24–72 h for immediate ART initiation.
patterns.ehr_plan: Acute HIV-1 infection — Ag/Ab screen reactive, HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation negative (antibody-negative window), HIV-1 RNA detected. Plan: HIV-1 quantitative viral load + CD4; resistance genotype; baseline CMP/CBC/LFTs/lipids/glucose/UA; full STI co-infection screen (RPR + treponemal, HBV serology, HCV, GC/CT NAAT all-site, trichomonas if female); latent TB (IGRA); pregnancy test if applicable; urgent preventive medicine consult — recent exposed contacts should be evaluated for PEP within 72 h; HIV-specialty referral within 24–72 h for immediate ART. Counsel on very high transmissibility during acute phase.
patterns.patient_communication: Your blood test shows a very recent HIV infection. People are most contagious in the first weeks of HIV, so it is important to start treatment as soon as possible — we will get you to an HIV specialist within the next 1 to 3 days to begin medication. Use protection (or abstinence) until you are on treatment with an undetectable viral load. We will test you for other sexually transmitted infections and tuberculosis. The preventive medicine team will help with notifying recent contacts — anyone exposed in the last 72 hours can take a preventive medication (PEP) to lower their risk of becoming infected.

patterns.label: False-reactive screen — likely not HIV (repeat to confirm)
patterns.category: Reactive 4th-gen screen with negative confirmatory differentiation AND negative HIV-1 RNA — likely false-reactive screen
next_tests: Repeat 4th-gen Ag/Ab and HIV-1 RNA in 2–4 weeks to confirm clearance / rule out very early infection.
Review specimen handling and any cross-reactive conditions (recent vaccination, autoimmune disease, pregnancy).
If ongoing high-risk exposure, repeat at 4–6 and 12 weeks per CDC.
Continue full STI screen — false-reactive HIV does not rule out other STIs.
patterns.ehr_plan: Reactive 4th-gen HIV screen with NEGATIVE HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation AND NEGATIVE HIV-1 RNA — pattern consistent with false-reactive screen. Plan: repeat 4th-gen Ag/Ab + HIV-1 RNA in 2–4 weeks; review cross-reactive conditions; continue full STI panel; counsel that current evidence does not support HIV infection but confirmation is needed.
patterns.patient_communication: Your initial HIV screen was reactive but the follow-up tests (HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation and HIV-1 RNA) were both negative. This pattern usually means the first test was a false alarm. We will repeat both tests in 2 to 4 weeks to be sure. In the meantime, please complete the rest of the STI testing.

fallback.label: Indeterminate — repeat / send to lab for full algorithm completion
fallback.category: Indeterminate HIV pattern — confirmatory testing incomplete
next_tests: Complete the CDC laboratory testing algorithm: if 4th-gen Ag/Ab is reactive, the lab should reflex to HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation; if differentiation is indeterminate or negative, reflex to HIV-1 RNA.
Repeat 4th-gen Ag/Ab in 2–4 weeks if indeterminate persists.
fallback.ehr_plan: HIV testing incomplete or indeterminate. Plan: complete CDC algorithm (reactive 4th-gen → HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation → HIV-1 RNA if differentiation indeterminate / negative); repeat in 2–4 weeks if pattern persists; continue full STI panel.
fallback.patient_communication: We need to complete the rest of your HIV testing before we can interpret the result. We will arrange the follow-up tests now.

sources: CDC HIV Laboratory Testing Algorithm (2018, updated 2024)
DHHS Adult and Adolescent ARV Guidelines
